comments.text: Since $t_2(n)$ is at least about $\sqrt{n}$, I'm guessing there's a factor $1/N$ or so missing somewhere. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Yes, there are primes in $N/2<p<N$ and $t_2(p)=p-1$ so the sum must be $\Omega(N)$.
The sum must be $\Omega( N^2 / \log N)$ ;) Every term being at least $1$ already lead to a linear lower bound
See https://mathweb.ucsd.edu/~ronspubs/80_11_number_theory.pdf page 76. It is about the average, so the $\frac 1 N$ is indeed missing. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
But in that paper, it is written that Hall proved that the average goes to 0. Which is equivalent to the question as written here. So I think the error is present in the original paper too.

Right. That implies that a reference to Hall s work is also necessary to consider it fully solved... The statement here is indeed equivalent with the one written in the book, but the latter is incorrect. The average being $o(N)$ is not surprising (CRT and most numbers having many prime factors), but also not trivial...

Thanks all! I found the missing reference to be [ErHa78]. On reflection, and taking this new source into account, I think it's appropriate to have the 'main question' being the sharper upper bound. In particular this problem has moved from solved to open.
Up to a shift by one (for $a(n) \not=1$), this is https://oeis.org/A344005. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
